text: I've been tasked to discuss laparoscopic left hepatectomy. So I have no disclosures. This is the outline of my presentation. I give you a brief introduction. We talk about patient port positioning, mobilization of the left liver, key steps in the ultrasound, the portal dissection, the chymotranssection, how we divide the left hepatic vein, and I give you some important points at the end.

So if we look at this data here from our friend Ruben, then we find that the left hepatectomy is actually less common than, I would say, a more complicated right hepatectomy. It's the fifth most performed liver resection. And if you look at the data comparing lab versus open for major resection in this recent study, we see that the postoperative outcomes seem favorable. One word of caution in this session that is very technical. In the Western world, I think a left hepatectomy should be an uncommon procedure. If you look at colorectal liver metastasis that are located in the left liver, and we look at the group of patients that were resected with a parenchymal sparing versus non-parenchymal sparing approach. There's no difference actually in outcome the first time around. But colorectal liver metastasis is a chronic disease and three out of four patients will recur. And the ability to rescue this recurrence goes away if you're wasteful with the left liver. So you can see here the data for month after the hepatectomy for the parenchymal sparing approach versus the non-parenchymal sparing approach.

The patient is positioned in the French position for a left hepatectomy, with the surgeon standing between the patient's legs. The first port is placed midway between the rib cage and the umbilicus, with a preference for a higher position to accommodate the liver fulcrum. The setup is prepared for portal dissection and parenchymal transection, including the division of the left hepatic vein.

The surgeon begins mobilization of the liver by taking down the falciform ligament, with the active blade positioned towards the outside and the inactive blade towards the inside. The left triangular and coronary ligaments are also dissected. A metastasis is visible, centered over the middle hepatic vein, extending into the right liver segments eight and five.

The surgeon uses an ultrasound probe to examine the liver, focusing on the middle hepatic vein from the left side of the falciform ligament. The ultrasound image reveals the branches draining into the middle hepatic vein, providing a clear view necessary for accessing this vein during the procedure.

The surgeon performs portal dissection, guided by the middle hepatic vein, which aids in navigating the parenchymal transection. The focus is on avoiding type three bile duct anatomy to prevent devascularization of the right posterior portal pedicle during the division of the left bile duct.

The surgeon dissects the hepatic artery, focusing on the left hepatic artery, and follows an intraglyconian approach towards the portal confluence. The gallbladder has been removed, but the cystic duct is disconnected to lower the hilar plate. The right and left portal veins are exposed, ensuring the portal confluence is not narrowed.

The surgeon is devascularizing the caudate lobe while ensuring the caudate branches remain perfused. The portal vein is divided to facilitate later exposure of the bile ducts. The left hepatic vein is visible draining into the inferior vena cava (IVC), with the left liver positioned above and the caudate lobe below. The parenchymal transection of the left liver is underway.

The surgeon identifies the middle hepatic vein, which serves as a guide through the parenchymal transection. The transection begins at the peripheral areas and progresses towards the central region, following a convex approach to minimize blood pooling. The anatomical relationship between the biliary confluence and the middle hepatic vein is maintained throughout the procedure.

The surgeon begins the parenchymal transection with the middle hepatic vein visible towards the left, near the biliary confluence. As the transection progresses, the surgeon follows the middle hepatic vein, identifying and counting the draining veins, including V4B ventral and dorsal, and V4A, to minimize bleeding. The view culminates in preparation for staple division using an open book approach.

The surgeon performs liver transection on a chemo-damaged liver with adjuvant metastasis using the crush clamp technique and bipolar forceps. The bile duct is divided to facilitate the parenchymal transection, resulting in increased bleeding due to centripetal flow. The V-five drainage vein is visible on the right side of the liver, guiding the transection process.

The surgeon completes the parenchymal transection, revealing the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the right hepatic vein. The left hepatic vein is prepared for division, with precautions taken against stapler misfiring, including the use of sutures to facilitate the stapling process. The gallbladder is removed, and the specimen is extracted through a Pfannenstiel incision.

Okay, so we talked today about how to do a left hepatectomy, and I think that the left lobectomy for metastasis should be a rare procedure in order to preserve parenchyma. It is important to define the hyla in middle hepatic vein anatomy prior to surgery. and it is key to use intraoperative ultrasound to guide your parenchymal transection and also for identification of all the metastases. The middle hepatic vein should guide the parenchymal transection and a peripheral to central dissection approach helps you to expose the transection plan as you're moving through the liver. I would like to thank my team for their support and you for your attention. Thank you very much.
